speaker: interviewer
text: December 22, 1995. Kurt Greenhut, tape three. OK, Mr. Greenhut. We left off where you were trying to escape from this mess via Casablanca. Can-- describe the route you took to get to Casablanca.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we went back to Marseilles after-- after the Spanish wouldn't let us in. We went back to Marseilles, and then we took a ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the Spanish wouldn't let you in, you were trying to get to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to Lisbon. We wanted to get to Lisbon. That was our objective.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's where the ocean liner was waiting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the Spanish wouldn't let you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the contact you had with the Spanish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they just-- they-- they explained it and all. Says, look, you just can't come in. This is-- Franco-- Franco has-- has given us an order. We can't let any-- any-- any Jews come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they knew you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they-- how did they know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who else would-- who-- who else would-- would cross the border? I mean, except for the Spanish, the Spanish Republicans. But we didn't look like Spanish Republicans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then-- OK. So then you decided to go through Marseilles, is that what you're saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We went back to Marseilles. We got-- we got ourself a merchant ship. I guess my father had to pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the feeling among yourself and your father about getting caught either by, I guess, the Vichy government, or by the Germans that might have been in Vichy, France?

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't worried about it anymore. In other words, we--we took a chance. And-- and we-- we took risks, in other words. And-- so-- we were on, you know. We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel your chances were any better because you were-- spoke German and you were Germans? Or did that not factor in at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- believe me, no. It didn't-- it didn't-- I mean, I did speak some German in that-- in that Quaker home. But I tried French. I tried as much-- my French is very limited. But I-- I tried to speak French as much as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to get financing to do all this traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a lot to do with it. My mother-- see, that's where it helped. My mother being over in the United States was able to get money. She worked as a domestic. But she also got help from HIAS to get-- to-- to get us out of here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to communicate with him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with her during that period of time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then you made your way-- way to Marseilles. And what-- when was that? Do you remember the approximate time that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember. It-- in the fall of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the feeling in Marseilles when you got there about the war? And-- do you recall any feelings that you might have had or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I try to stay--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that the population--

speaker: interviewee
text: --away from people, you see. I was trying to stay away from people. I don't want to get involved in-- in-- having them recognize who I am-- recognize me, who I am. In other words, it-- it was my job to stay away from this-- from the crowd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that period of time that you were in Marseilles, did you come across anybody that could be characterized as-- as a notable historical figure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just Petain. He was there in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was Petain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marshal Petain. He was the-- he-- he was the president of-- of France at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the circumstances that you saw Marshal Petain under?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was-- he was-- he was parading. There was a parade or something, and-- and he was in a car. And-- and I guess he was visiting Marseilles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the people's reaction to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too bad, believe it or not. [LAUGHS] I mean, he's-- but what happened historically, of course, is different. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- they still loved him as a hero.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the hero of World War I, remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And how long were you in Marseilles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess-- let's say, probably another three months. In other words, let me see, September, October, November. About three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got ourselves some kind of apartment for the time being. An apartment. I don't know who paid for it, but it was an apartment. And wasn't much of an apartment. But we-- we able to-- to survive for three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you fearful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always fearful. Always fearful. The only time I really felt secure was when I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And under what circumstances did you leave Marseilles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, follow our plans. In other words, we were scheduled to be on that ocean liner. And-- and since we couldn't make it to go to Lisbon, we decided to-- to go another route. And we ended up in Casablanca. That's where we had to wait about three weeks before-- before that ship came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from Marseilles to Casablanca?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- well, we took a-- a merchant ship. And we stop-- made several stops. And the British submarines protected us, believe it or not. The British submarines protected us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was on this ship? I mean, beside yourself and your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some other refugees. Some other German Jews, French Jews. You know, it's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other refugees--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you can recall who maybe weren't Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they had to be mostly Jewish-- Jewish people. People who had the money to pay. And the only thing I-- I-- we did exciting was playing chess. My father and myself, we-- we played a game of chess. We-- I learned how to play chess.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how long of a trip was this from-- approximately, from Marseilles to Casablanca?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. I don't remember now. But it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, it can't-- I mean, if it was-- it had to be about three weeks maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was that long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this a direct stop, or did you make stopovers on the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh,yes. We make stopover. Oran, Algiers, Tangiers. We stop-- made three stops before we ended up in Casablanca.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was Casablanca the place to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the-- was it the Free French that had-- that had control of Casablanca? Well, once you got to Casablanca, you were fairly safe. I mean, the-- well of course, Vichy, France-- the-- the Vichy French government was still in control of-- of-- of Casablanca. I-- I really don't know. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when you made these various stops on the way to Casablanca, was there any fear among yourself, or either you or your father, about being uncovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The only thing we were afraid of is that the German submarines would be in-- in-- would be stopping us in the Mediterranean. But the British took care of that. Remember, that was during the day when Rommel was moving-- was-- was having-- was doing very well in-- in Africa-- North Africa. So-- and it was during that time, period of time. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we just-- we were just lucky, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when you got to Casablanca, can you describe what occurred at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing much. We stayed there for about three weeks. We-- we visited the-- the Jewish, the new Jewish Quarters and the old Jewish Quarters. And the-- the new Jewish Quarter was not bad at all. And-- but we stayed outside of Casablanca. We had-- we had camped outside. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whoever was-- in other words, whoever went with us with the-- with the merchant ship was with us too. And-- and sure enough, I don't remember now exactly what day it was, but the-- the ocean liner from Lisbon came and took us aboard. And then with another trip went-- we went to-- our trip led us to Jamaica and Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the name of the ship that brought you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Serpa Pinto. The Serpa Pinto. I-- I guess it's a-- I'm not sure what the translation of it is. But it's-- what is it, a horse? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, anyway. Serpa Pinto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did your father make all these arrangements to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: --get on this ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. My father made all the arrangements. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever related to you how he was able to make these arrangements?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it ever related to you-- did he ever relate to you how he was able to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All-- all I know is that I-- you know, I know my father made a mistake when he didn't get out in time. But, since then, since he came back from Dachau, he was a very resourceful person. And I--I tell you the truth, I think I--I owe my life to my father. I really owe my life to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were home free when you got to Casablanca?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I mean, not home free, but I-- we felt--

speaker: interviewer
text: A sense of relief?

speaker: interviewee
text: Relief, right. And after that, it was basically just a matter of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe this ship that took you from Casablanca to Jamaica, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's not like the-- those modern cruise lines, you know. The modern cruises, you know. It's-- it was-- it was an ocean liner, but it was a small one. And I guess [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had play room-- play-- play room, you know. They had a place to eat, a restaurant. One restaurant and one game room. But it was a small-- a small ocean liner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how many people were on that ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember exactly. But I would say that there were about-- see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 300.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they mostly Jews, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any talk about the war and the plight of the Jews at that time, that you can recall, during this trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In other words, it-- it just didn't occur to me. I mean, I-- again, I played my game of chess with my father to pass the time. But we were-- what-- what we knew later on is that a few months later, all the Jews in southern France were deported to Eastern Europe. And that's what we-- what we were able to avoid. And if we would have stayed there a few more months, we would have been deported as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Jamaica. Is that what you're saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jamaica, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was your first stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jamaica. The British treated us well. The British treated us very well. And it was Cuba where-- in Cuba that they wouldn't let us go on board--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how long were you in Jamaica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actually-- did you enter the country of Jamaica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we didn't stay in a-- in a big-- in a city. We stayed in a-- in a-- the British had some kind of a camp for us. And they kept us there until-- until the ship came, until it was time to-- to go on board again.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then our trip was to Cuba.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the government would not let us in. I mean, they wouldn't let us even board or go and-- and roam around or whatever. You know, we had to stay on the ship. So it was just a matter of a day or two before we moved on to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what happened when you arrived in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- my mother was there. You know, in other words, it was a-- quite a traumatic experience again. It was very--a very happy experience. And we were on-- on the way of-- of becoming part of the American scene, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to communicate with your mother, or your father was able to communicate with your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe this reunion and-- and what happened when you entered into New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a-- a lot of our relatives were there. And for a few days, we stayed with them. But my mother, remember, worked in-- in the Boston area. So my-- and because my mother worked in the Boston area, my father and I also moved to that area, and we started a new life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many relatives. Can you describe some of the relatives that were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- there were a lot of relatives. A lot of cousins, by the way. But we had really, to tell you the truth, nothing in common with them, because it's-- they-- they were still in their old way of living. You know, in other words, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they-- I wanted to be away. I mean, my father and I wanted to be away and start a new life. And this was not going to-- if we would have stayed in New York, we would have-- we would have fallen into-- into their way of life. And we wanted-- when we came to United States, we said we wanted it completely new. In other words, I was looking for assimilation. And-- and the-- my relatives over in New York were-- were back in the old days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was early 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- yeah. February, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was the last time you had seen your mother before you saw her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the last time--

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I saw her was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was over three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was three years. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Either--

speaker: interviewee
text: '40, '41. About two and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that either you or your father had seen the wife and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were-- it was quite a-- a reunion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And then where did you say you went from there? You went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to Massachusetts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, outside Boston. Called Winthrop, Massachusetts. And-- and that's where I stayed for-- for the-- for the-- the next 10 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did you do when you got there? Did you speak English at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I had-- I know I had to learn the English language. And I made it my business to make sure I-- I-- I did learn. And I had special-- well, special attention. I had a special teacher who taught me how to speak, how to get rid of my accent, and whatever. And I was determined to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to the time of you commencing your education in this country, when was the last time you had any type of formal education?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it had been like seven years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years. Seven years. And it's just like-- the only education I got was basically real life experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And learning to play chess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- oh, and chess. That's right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So describe this education that you got in this new country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I-- I had a sort of a rough beginning, because they put me in a-- I think the fifth grade, you know, where I should have been much higher. But I got some double promotions. But still, I-- I graduated high school at age 20. But it didn't make any difference, because when I went to college I had a lot of students that were my age, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the reaction of your contemporaries at that time to having this German immigrant in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm always considered--

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the German refugee over in-- in-- in Winthrop, Massachusetts, you know. I mean, they accepted me. The Jewish population accepted me. But the-- and I became very active, very quickly. But I had some problems with the-- like the McLaughlin gang, you know, that they--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is that? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were a whole bunch of 12 to 13 cousins, and I guess they-- they didn't want any stranger, or they-- they thought I was a Nazi or something, you know. So they-- they gave me a hard time. I once remember I had to run for my life from those kids, you know. They were kind of-- they were pretty rough. And they threw rocks at us and all that business-- at me. But I had already made some friends. So they were in the same-- they had the same problem that I had because they were supporting me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism there, or was it just anti-German feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right-- at that point, it was anti-German. I think anti-- I was a-- I was-- you know, they were not-- probably not smart enough to realize that I was-- I was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that? I mean, the fact that you were actually being persecuted to some extent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by being--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well,I knew it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --being German while the Germans were persecuting you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a temporary situation. I mean, once I-- I got away from-- from high school and into college, I met a lot of people that were concerned and-- and were sympathetic to my--to my-- way I feel, the way I thought and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up in Germany, if I could digress a-- many, many years, was there ever any thought, either yourself or your family, about coming to America? I'm talking about before Hitler's rise to power.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I can say is that I was-- the-- the-- the poorest subject that I had in school [LAUGHS] was English. I-- I got bad grades in English. Everything else was fine, but English. In other words, I was not concerned-- I never thought for a minute that I was going to end up in-- in the United States. That's-- that's what-- you know. So you get-- you get an idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And after you graduated college-- excuse me, high school-- you said that you graduated when you were 20 years old. What year was that, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, high school-- I graduated high school 1948. And-- and I got good grades, by the way. I started getting good grades the-- the last four years-- 9th, 10th, 11th, 12th. So I was pretty good. And I-- I had choices of colleges. And I thought the best thing-- best place to go was Tufts College--

speaker: interviewer
text: By--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Medford, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, in-- at the end of the war in 1945, what was your feeling at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was delighted. I was extremely happy. I mean, thank goodness. Now-- you know, now I can start concentrating on-- on my life. Oh, absolutely. You know, when-- when people made bonfires in-- in 1945? I was very, very delighted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you aware of what happened to some of your family? Did you start evaluating it at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became aware that many of my-- my-- my family didn't make it. In other words, as I said, about half of them did not make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any that you could recall specifically that you grew up with that you never saw after you left Karlsruhe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my father had another brother, called Emil, who-- who did-- who ended up in a concentration camp, and I was very close to him. And unfortunate. Very unfortunate. He-- he didn't make it. He ended up in-- in Eastern Europe. One of the camps in Eastern Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were others that-- cousins, uncles that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --died in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Europe during that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And I was very upset about it. But you know, life is for the living. In other words, you've got to start concentrating on-- on the living. And I made it my business to-- to do something with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And-- and-- and you mentioned that you went on to Tufts after you graduated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --high school. What made you decide on Tufts, which is in Boston.

speaker: interviewee
text: Medford, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a teacher in high school that-- that was sort of sponsoring me. And it was a good university. So-- I was a good student, you know,so it was just a matter of-- he was sponsoring me. And he said, why don't you go to Tufts, because I went to Tufts. And I said, well, I'll take-- I mean, I knew very little about colleges at that time. So I knew about Harvard and-- and Yale and all that. But I was very pleased. I was very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living home or did you live on campus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was commuting. I was commuting. In other words, from my house. In other words, I was not living on-- on the campus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when I went to Columbia University the next year-- I mean, after-- at '52 to '53--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you transfer from Tufts to Columbia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you got your B.A.

speaker: interviewee
text: --graduated. I graduated with a-- a B.A. in-- in History. And then I went to Columbia University, who accepted me. And then I got my Masters in Columbia University.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were-- you said you were a B.A. in History, Was there any particular specialty that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. European History and American History.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel your war experiences and growing up in Germany was helpful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that what precipitated you to-- to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: --major in History, do you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I'm not sure what exactly-- what made-- what my-- what exactly made me do this. But the point is this, that history was my favorite subject, OK? Maybe it-- all my travels and everything else had something to do with it. But history became my-- my favorite subject, and that-- I stuck with it. American history, European history. And later on, you know, when I start teaching history, I even included Middle East and Medieval.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what-- what caused you go to Columbia? Was this for-- to get a Masters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a Masters. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you get a Masters in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Political Science. The Masters was in Political Science. I was interested in government.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Columbia?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just one year. Yes, New York. Columbia in one year. I made it in one year, the Masters. And then I went to the service for the Korean War. That was between 1953 and '55. And then I met my wife during that period. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we got married. Well, I was mostly in-- in the-- in the shipping area. In other words, I was shipping soldiers in-- out and in, you know. Receiving them. Soldiers that were-- came from all parts of-- all parts of the world. And-- and I also took care of the finances. The finances with the-- you know, how much these soldiers were getting, and everything else. I would-- I would process those things. I think the-- the reason that I never went overseas, basically, is because I met my wife during that time. And when we got married--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1954. We-- when we came-- when I came back, I had less than a year to go. I didn't pay the-- Uncle Sam to-- to-- pay me-- pay them to go over to Korea. Besides, 1953, a truce was signed anyway. But no one knew exactly how long that truce was going to last.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian. Lillian.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met her during a-- during a dance in-- in-- in the Fort, in-- in Fort Dix. And it was love at first sight. I mean, we just had a proper chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you reside and where did you make your initial marital abode?

speaker: interviewee
text: Asbury Park, New Jersey. In other words, when I-- when I got out, my first job was in Asbury Park, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a teacher, and-- and that's where we lived together. In other words--

speaker: interviewer
text: At what level did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: High school. High school. And they-- they also needed a-- a German teacher, so it worked out nicely. History and German. See, I-- I had a-- a minor in-- in German. That's, of course, basically, from-- from my experience. But it-- it worked out very nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you taught both subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your-- was your wife involved in any vocational activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was a secretary of-- in a South Philadelphia school. Secretary. But when she-- when she moved to Asbury Park, she wasn't doing anything except taking care that-- of my daughter, who was-- you know, was just one-- let me see. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was just about a year old, my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she the first born?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your daughter the first born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The first born, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda. Linda Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: October, 1955.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other children besides Linda?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linda? Yeah. I have Steven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is 35. And-- now, 35. And Scott, who's 26, who's getting married in February.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there are any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandchildren? Yes. I have three of them. One is Erin, and-- and Laura, and Diana. Laura and Diana are-- belong to my son, Steven. And-- and Erin is my-- my daughter's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.
